coredata.dc:title: Off-on fluorescent switching of boron-doped carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive sensing of catechol and glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Y.
Chen, A.Y.
Huang, Y.Y.
He, X.
Xie, X.F.
He, B.
Yang, J.H.
Wang, X.Y.
coredata.dc:description: A signal-off-on probe of B-doped carbon quantum dot (B-CQD) is developed for ultrasensitive detection of catechol (CC) by fluorescence quenching and glutathione (GSH) by fluorescence recovering without any auxiliary of biomolecules or labeled materials. In this sensing system, the B-CQDs with a high quantum yield of 42% are prepared via a facile hydrothermal method with precursors of citric acid as C source and sodium tetraphenylborate as B source. The microstructure and fluorescence stability of the B-CQDs are investigated. Under the optimum conditions, the B-CQDs are highly sensitive for detecting CC with a linear range of 1–50 nM and a detection limit of 0.25 nM under a typical signal-to-noise ratio of 3 to 1 (S/N = 3), and for sensing GSH with a linear range of 2–100 nM and a detection limit of 0.5 nM (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, the developed B-CQDs show a good selectivity for sensing CC and GSH under high concentrations of interfering substances, such as biological thiols and amino acids. The B-CQDs are successfully applied to determination of CC in river water and GSH in human serum, where the recoveries are 103.3%–106.0% and 99.8%–106.3% for CC and GSH sensing, respectively. The signal-off-on mechanisms of the B-CQDs sensor are discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B-doped carbon quantum dot
Fluorescent sensing
Catechol
Glutathione
Sensitivity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Program of Shanghai Academic Research Leader
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of precursors (a), molar ratio of precursors (b), hydrothermal temperature (c), and hydrothermal time (d) on the QY of the B-CQDs. The insets in (a–d) are the corresponding UV–Vis and PL spectra. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Microstructure of the B-CQDs. (a) UV–Vis and PL spectra; (b) Raman spectrum; (c) Fluorescence lifetime curve; (d) TEM image; (e) HRTEM image; (f) AFM image. The insets in Fig. 2a are the pictures of B-CQDs solution under visible light and UV illumination. The inset in Fig. 2f is the height profile along the indicated line AB. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of the B-CQDs. (a) Survey spectrum; (b) B 1s; (c) Schematic of B-CQDs structure; (d) C 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Effects of pH (a), temperature (b), UV illumination time (c) and storage time (d) on the PL intensity of the B-CQDs and CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence quenching performance of CC toward B-CQDs in 0.1 M PBS. (a) Variation of fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs and CQDs with staying time after the addition of CC (200 nM); (b) PL spectra of B-CQDs containing CC with different concentrations; (c) PL response of B-CQDs with concentration of CC; (d) Interference experiments. The inset in Fig. 5c is the PL response relationship of CC toward B-CQDs and CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence recovering performance of B-CQDs solution containing CC by GSH in 0.1 M PBS. (a) Fluorescence recovering performance with staying time; (b) PL response of the mixture of B-CQDs and CC (200 nM) with different concentration of GSH; (c) Interference experiment; (d) Cyclic detection of CC and GSH by B-CQDs, where the signal-off-on sensing performance is repeated for the second round. The inset in Fig. 6b is the PL spectra of B-CQDs containing CC after the addition of GSH.

Effects of molecular structures on fluorescence performance of B-CQDs. (a) O-benzoquinone; (b) GSSH and N2H4; (c) Homogenous biomolecules of oxalic acid, Gly, C12H26S, Cys and Hcy; (d) Concentration and addition sequence of GSH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic of the signal-off-on sensing mechanisms of B-CQDs toward CC and GSH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparisons of the sensing systems for the detection of CC.
Analytical results of CC in 200-fold diluted river water samples using B-CQDs.
Comparisons of the fluorescence sensing GSH.
Analytical results of GSH in 100-fold diluted human serum samples using B-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Off-on fluorescent switching of boron-doped carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive sensing of catechol and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, 200050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Anesthesiology and SICU, Xinhua Hospital, School of Medicine, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Anesthesiology and SICU, Xinhua Hospital, School of Medicine, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A signal-off-on probe of B-doped carbon quantum dot (B-CQD) is developed for ultrasensitive detection of catechol (CC) by fluorescence quenching and glutathione (GSH) by fluorescence recovering without any auxiliary of biomolecules or labeled materials. In this sensing system, the B-CQDs with a high quantum yield of 42% are prepared via a facile hydrothermal method with precursors of citric acid as C source and sodium tetraphenylborate as B source. The microstructure and fluorescence stability of the B-CQDs are investigated. Under the optimum conditions, the B-CQDs are highly sensitive for detecting CC with a linear range of 1–50 nM and a detection limit of 0.25 nM under a typical signal-to-noise ratio of 3 to 1 (S/N = 3), and for sensing GSH with a linear range of 2–100 nM and a detection limit of 0.5 nM (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, the developed B-CQDs show a good selectivity for sensing CC and GSH under high concentrations of interfering substances, such as biological thiols and amino acids. The B-CQDs are successfully applied to determination of CC in river water and GSH in human serum, where the recoveries are 103.3%–106.0% and 99.8%–106.3% for CC and GSH sensing, respectively. The signal-off-on mechanisms of the B-CQDs sensor are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescent probes have been widely used to detect biomolecules, such as biological thiols of glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and phenol [1,2]. GSH plays a crucial role in maintaining the normal redox balance in living bodies [3], and abnormal levels of GSH can lead to cancer, Alzheimer’s and mitochondrial diseases [4]. Catechol (CC), the most important derivative of phenol, exhibits an extremely large threat to the safety of humans and animals because of the high toxicity, difficulty in degradation, and even carcinogenicity [5]. Umezawa et al. [6] reported a reversible organic fluorescent probe with a quantum yield (QY) of only 0.03% for detecting the intracellular GSH, where the fluorescent probe is synthesized by a series of Suzuki coupling reactions and chemical substitution reactions with Rhodamine as a staring material. Deng et al. [7] developed a novel fluorescent probe based on the combination of lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles and manganese dioxide nanosheets to determine GSH, where the limit of detection (LOD) was up to 900 nM. Most of the above fluorescent probes, inorganic or organic molecules, have the disadvantages of complicated chemical synthesis, extremely low QY (<1%), and low sensitivity [6–8].

As newly developed nano-fluorescent probes, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with particle sizes less than 10 nm exhibit unique superiority in biomolecular detection due to their good water dispersibility, photo-stability, high QY, and simple synthesis [9]. Compared with the pure CQDs/GQDs, heteroatom doping can further increase the QY and endow CQDs/GQDs with various new or improved structural, optical, and physicochemical properties [10,11]. The synthesis and fluorescence performance of the doped CQDs/GQDs, such as O, N, S doping [12–15], or N, S and S, P co-doping [16,17], have been widely investigated. Other GQDs modified by metal ions and rare earth ions are also developed [18,19]. These doped or modified GQD-based fluorescent probes have been applied for determining CC [20,21] and GSH [19,22]. For example, Li et al. successfully synthesized N-doped CQDs using C3N4 and ethanediamine as precursors to detect CC by fluorescence quenching [20]. Although they obtained N-CQDs with a QY high up to 28.5%, the LOD is not very satisfactory (close to 300 nM). Huang et al. [19] developed bis (3-pyridylmethyl) amine-functionalized CQDs conjugating with Cu (II) or Ag (I) as fluorescent probes to detect GSH, but the synthesis process is very complicated. Furthermore, the element-doped CQDs/GQDs as sensing probes meet some problems, such as poor chemical stability, repeatability, and undefined fine chemical groups by different precursors [9,11,20,21].

Different from the other doping elements (oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, etc.), B atom exhibits a quite lower electronegativity than C atom [23]. Thus, the doped B atoms in CQDs (B-CQDs) could lead to a positive charge distribution around C atoms, which facilitates the adsorption of biomolecules, as predicted by simulations [23–25]. In previous reports, boric hydroxyl groups modified on the surface of nanoparticles, such as CQDs and semiconductor quantum dots, can significantly enhance the sensitivity of dopamine (DA) and glucose (GS) analysis according to the fluorescence quenching phenomenon [26–29]. Ge et al. prepared B-GQDs by using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and borax in sodium hydroxide under hydrothermal process, and they found the B-GQDs can be served as the fluorescence probes towards Fe3+ ion and phosphate [30]. Therefore, B-CQDs can be expected to be more beneficial for sensing biomolecules, and environmental-friendly, efficient and scalable synthesis of B-CQDs with a high QY is highly desirable. In this work, we prepared B-CQDs for sensitively and selectively sensing CC by signal-off and GSH by signal-on methods. B-CQDs (QY up to 42%) were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) and sodium tetraphenylborate (NaTPB) as precursors. The sensing performances of B-CQDs to CC and GSH are investigated, and the results demonstrate that B-CQDs can be used as fluorescent probes for sensing CC and GSH with extremely low LOD, good selectivity, and stability.

Potassium hydroxide (KOH), dipotassium phosphate (K2HPO3), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO3), sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S·9H2O), urea (Ur), glycine (Gly), ascorbic acid (AA), β-phenethylamine (PEA), Cys, CA, GS, CC, CCl4 and GSH were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Rhodamine-6G (R6G), uric acid (UA), phenol, DA, Hcy and NaTPB were purchased from Sigma (Shanghai, China). Borax (Na2B4O7·10H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), hydrazine (N2H2), oxalic acid, dodecyl mercaptan (C12H26S), phenol, and boric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. The water used throughout experiments was purified by Millipore system with a resistivity of 18 MΩ cm−1. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by mixing the stock solutions of 0.2 M KH2PO4 and 0.2 M K2HPO4 containing 0.1 M KCl (pH 7.4).

7 mmol CA and different boron sources (NaTPB, borax, boric acid) with different mole ratio were dissolved in KOH solution with a total volume of 35 ml, and then sonicated for 30 min. The molar ratio of CA to KOH was 3:1. The mixture was transferred to a 50 ml PPL-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 140–180 °C for a certain time in a heating furnace. After cooling to room temperature, the synthesized B-CQDs were purified by extraction method to remove the impurities, where the CCl4 was selected as an organic extractant with a volume ratio of 1:1 to distilled water. After purification, the pure B-CQDs are obtained in distilled water. As a blank counterpart, the CQDs were produced under the same process conditions without the B precursor.

The microstructure analysis was performed on a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The foil for TEM observation was prepared as follows: the B-CQDs solution was firstly ultrasonic dispersed in ethanol for 20 min, and then dropped onto a Cu mesh (T200 Cu) with an ultrathin carbon film of 1–3 nm thick. The above B-CQDs solution was spin-coated on the silicon wafer surface, and the morphology was observed by atomic force microscope (AFM, Shimadzu SPM9700) operating at a semi-tapping mode in randomly selected zones. The UV–Vis spectrum was recorded on a Lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerKinElmer). The photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed using a Shimadzu RF-5301 Fluorescence spectrophotometer. Raman scattering spectrum was obtained using Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer with 532 nm laser. The time-resolved fluorescence spectrum was collected with Edinburgh FLSP920 steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a ThermoFisher-250XI X-ray electron spectrometer with focused monochromatized Al Kα radiation. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded by using a SPECTRUM 100 PerkinElmer spectrophotometer. The QY of the B-CQDs was measured via the reference of R6G in ethanol, where the QY of the reference is 98% [15].

The B-CQDs were diluted by PBS to obtain a starting matrix (12 μg ml −1). The starting solutions of CC (4 μM) and GSH (4 μM) were prepared in water and the other concentrations of CC and GSH were obtained by the dilution of the starting solution with PBS buffer solution. After staying 25 min, the PL of the B-CQDs with the addition of CC was measured at 443 nm excited by 350 nm. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the B-CQDs sensor to CC, the PL spectra of the mixture with co-existing interferences are measured, including Ur, UA, AA, GS, phenolic compounds (phenol, PEA, DA), amino acids (Gly, Cys, Hcy), some metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+), and anions (Cl−, S2−). The concentration of the interfering substances and CC are 200 nM and 40 nM, respectively. When detecting the GSH by fluorescence recovering, the starting mixture contains B-CQDs (12 μg ml −1) and CC (200 nM). The PL of the mixed solution was measured at 443 nm excited by 350 nm after staying 30 min for sensing GSH. Similarly, the interfering experiments in detecting GSH by B-CQDs with CC were performed by the addition of some typical biomolecules in the serum, such as Na+, Cl−, S2−, Ur, UA, AA, GS, phenol, PEA, DA, and Gly. The concentration of each interfering substance was 200 nM and that of the GSH was 100 nM. CC in the river water from Shanghai Environmental Monitoring Station was diluted 200-fold with PBS buffer solution without any other pretreatment. Fresh human serum was collected from Center Medical Hospital (Shanghai, China). The serum samples were pretreated according to the method reported before [8]. The specimens were diluted 100-fold with PBS buffer solution and then spiked with different concentrations of GSH. The concentrations of CC and GSH were determined according to the standard addition method. All tests for sensing CC and GSH were performed at 23 °C.

When evaluating the effects of molecular structure on fluorescence performance of B-CQDs, the PL spectra of the B-CQDs containing CC (200 nM) before and after oxidization by 30% H2O2 (1 ml) were recorded. Similarly, the PL intensities of the B-CQDs after the addition of N2H2 (100 nM) and GSSH (50 nM) oxidized by 30% H2O2 (1 ml) from GSH (100 nM) were tested. As comparisons, the PL spectra of the B-CQDs containing CC (200 nM) after the addition of N2H2 (100 nM) and GSSH (50 nM) were also determined. The PL curves of the B-CQDs solution containing homologous molecules of GSH with and without CC (200 nM) were recorded, including oxalic acid, Gly, C12H26S, Cys and Hcy, where the concentrations of all analytes were 100 nM. The addition of GSH into the mixture of B-CQDs and CC denotes as CC + GSH, while the mixture of GSH and CC added into B-CQDs solution is referred to as (CC + GSH)*. The term, “1/2GSH”, means the concentration of GSH is 50 nM, half of the initial GSH concentration (100 nM).

The B-CQDs are produced by considering different precursors and hydrothermal parameters, as shown in Fig. 1 . Compared with the blank CQDs and B-CQDs prepared by borax and boric acid, B-CQDs by NaTPB as a precursor have an obvious absorption peak at 350 nm (inset in Fig. 1a), which originates from n - π* transition of C–B [20,21,31]. Corresponding, the PL peak of the B-CQDs by NaTPB shows the highest intensity at 443 nm (inset in Fig. 1a), and the highest QY up to 27%, more than three times of the other B-CQDs and blank CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1a. In order to optimize the hydrothermal reaction by using NaTPB as a precursor, the reaction parameters are modified, such as mole ratio of precursors (Fig. 1b), hydrothermal temperature (Fig. 1c), and reaction time (Fig. 1d). The results show that the B-CQDs have a QY up to 42% under the conditions of CA and NaTPB as precursors with a molar ratio of 14:1 at 180 °C for 8 h, which is much higher than the B-free CQDs counterpart (6.3%). When selecting B-CQDs as sensors, the QY is preferentially considered, thus, B-CQDs with the highest QY prepared at this processing are used in the following work.

The as-prepared B-GQDs emit a blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 2 a. An independent PL behavior is observed at the excited wavelength of 350–380 nm (Fig. 2a), indicating a stable emission performance. Additionally, the maximum excitation wavelength is at 350 nm, which is coincided with the UV–Vis absorption peak at 350 nm, as shown in Fig. 2a. The Raman spectrum of the B-CQDs can be resolved into three peaks of 1352, 1520, and 1573 cm−1 (Fig. 2b), correspondingly to disordered (D), amorphous (C) and crystalline (G) bands, respectively [32,33]. The average lifetime of the B-CQDs is 4.0 ns (Fig. 2c). The TEM image reveals that the B-CQDs have uniform particle sizes (Fig. 2d), and the average size is estimated to 8 nm. High-resolution (HRTEM) image shows a graphene lattice (Fig. 2e), and the particle sizes distribute in a major range of 7–10 nm. The AFM image (Fig. 2f) also gives the spherical morphologies of the B-CQDs particles, where the heights are in the range of 1–4 nm (inset in Fig. 2f).

The chemical compositions of the B-CQDs is analyzed by XPS, as shown in Fig. 3 . In survey spectrum of Fig. 3a, three peaks at 191.0, 288.0, and 535.0 eV correspond to B 1s, C 1s, and O 1s, respectively, indicating that B atoms are doped into the CQDs by the hydrothermal reaction. B 1s spectrum can be resolved into four peaks, i.e. B4C at 187.8 eV, BC3 at 189.1 eV, BC2O at 190.4 eV, and BCO2 at 191.7 eV, as given in Fig. 3b [33,34]. The resolved structure of B-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 3c. The total B content is 0.88% (at%), and the BCO2 structure has the highest content (0.46%). The small doping amount of B atoms in B-CQDs originates from electron-deficient feature of B atom, which possesses quite different electronegativity (2.04 for B vs 2.55 for C), resulting in a difficult doping into the CQDs framework [23,28]. The devolution analysis of C 1s spectrum also shows the C–B structure at 283.5 eV, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3d [33,35]. The other chemical structures are fitted, such as C–C&CC at 284.6 eV, C–O&C–O–C at 286.0 eV, CO at 287.4 eV, COOH at 288.5 eV [30,36], as given in Fig. 3d. The as-prepared B-CQDs also contain a few impurities of the K element from solvent (KOH) and Na from the precursor of NaTPB, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S1. FTIR spectra (Supplementary Fig. S2a) further confirm the presence of oxygen-containing groups (-OH, COO−) at 3425, 1577, and 1394 cm−1 both in the bare CQDs and B-CQDs [37]. Characteristic peaks of B-doping are observed at 1420, 1350, 1192, 1010, and 1116 cm−1 (Supplementary Fig. S2b), corresponding to the B–O stretching vibration, B–O–H bending vibration, and C–B stretching vibration, respectively [28,31,38]. These results are consistent with the XPS analysis.

The enhanced PL intensity are obtained in all the B-CQDs, although the doping content of B atoms is small. The higher QY of B-CQDs can be attributed to be the hybridization structure of the surface chemical states and the connected core. All B-doped CQDs specimens have similar UV–Vis absorption curves and PL spectra independent of B sources (insets in Fig. 1a). Additionally, the maximum excitation wavelength is consistent with the absorption peak of UV–Vis spectra at 350 nm, and the higher absorption peak produces stronger PL intensity (insets in Fig. 1a–d). These results indicate that the B doping produces a strong transition of electrons [24,25]. Furthermore, a wavelength-independent behavior of the B-CQDs by NaTPB is observed at the excited wavelength of 350–380 nm (Fig. 2a), implying that the fluorescence primarily originates from the hybridized surface chemical groups [28]. These active surface states, such as BCO2, BC2O from XPS analysis in Fig. 3b, are formed in the hydrothermal process accompanied with the carbonization reactions of CA [23]. Generally, heteroatom doping, involving O, N, S, P doping [12–17], and B doping in this work, can significantly enhance the QY. The underlying mechanisms are diverse and complicated, and further studies are needed.

When choosing quantum dots as sensors, the dominant problems are the low QY and instability [10,11,20]. However, the as-prepared B-CQDs exhibit not only high QY (42%) but also a good stability. The effect of pH, temperature, UV illumination, and storage time on the fluorescence performance are given in Fig. 4 , where the F0 is the starting fluorescence intensity of the newly produced solution tested at pH = 7.4 and 23 °C. The B-CQDs exhibit a steady fluorescence intensity at pH = 6–12 with a max relative error of 5% (Fig. 4a), better than the bare CQDs with a max relative error of 14%. Interesting, the B-CQDs have a much better temperature stability at 0–70 °C, while the blank CQDs exhibit a significant thermo-sensitivity with significant PL decrease when the temperature is higher than 40 °C (Fig. 4b). When illuminating the B-CQDs and CQDs solution by UV beam, the B-CQDs present a better bleaching resistance, and retain approximately constant after illuminating 10 min (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the B-CQDs can be stocked up to 6 months with basically constant PL intensity (Fig. 4d). Thus, the B-CQDs exhibit a much better endurance of pH, temperature, UV illumination, and storage time than the blank CQDs. The good stability of the B-CQDs ensures the reliability and practicability in the biomolecule sensing tests.

B-CQDs are firstly applied to sense CC based on a fluorescence-quenching phenomenon of CC to B-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) is used to reflect the fluorescence change before and after the addition of CC, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of initial B-CQDs solution, and F is that of B-CQDs solution containing CC. Fig. 5 a clearly shows that CC (200 nM) exhibit no quenching effect to the pure CQDs, but a significant quenching effect to B-CQDs. Thus, the quenching performance of CC to B-CQDs can be attributed to the B-doping effect. The effects of different types of B-CQDs prepared by different precursors and molar ratio on the PL change are provided in Supplementary Fig. S3. After linear fitting, the slopes of the B-CQDs prepared by NaTPB, boric acid, and borax as precursors are −13.2, −7.4, and −5.9, respectively (see Supplementary Fig. S3a). The B-CQDs by NaTPB show the steepest slope, thereafter, have the highest sensitivity. Additionally, the B-CQDs by NaTPB and CA with a molar ratio of 14:1 are optimal, resulting from the significant change of slope (see Supplementary Fig. S3b). According to PL change in Fig. 5b, a good linear relationship of F/F0 with concentration of CC is obtained in the range of 1–50 nM, as shown in Fig. 5c and the inset. The linear regression equation is
F /
F
0
= 0.9985 − 0.00978
C
c c
( nM )
. The estimated correlation coefficient, R2, is 0.9999. The LOD of CC is 0.25 nM under a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 to 1 (S/N = 3), which is greatly lower than these of the recent results, see Table 1 [20,21,39–48].

The selectivity of the B-CQDs sensor to CC are carried out at five-fold concentration of interferences than CC (in Fig. 5d). The co-existing interferences, such as Ur, UA, AA, GS, phenolic compounds (phenol, PEA, DA), some metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+) and anions (Cl−, S2−) display a negligible effect on the PL intensity with a relative error less than 2%. While Hcy shows the max relative error of 5.0% among the three types of amino acids (Gly, Cys, Hcy). The developed B-CQDs probe is employed to detect the CC in river water by standard addition method. After 200-fold dilution, the quantitative recoveries of the spiked CC range from 103.3% to 106.0%, as shown in Table 2 . The relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 5.3%.

From the above results, the CC can seriously quench the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs, however, the fluorescence intensity quenched by CC is gradually recovered after the addition of GSH and reaches constant after 30 min, as shown in Fig. 6 a. With the increase of GSH concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution of B-CQDs containing CC (200 nM) increases, see the inset in Fig. 6b. A good linear relationship is fitted at the concentration of 2–100 nM (Fig. 6b). The linear regression equation is
F /
F
0
= 0.15 + 0.00489
C
G S H
( nM )
. The LOD of GSH sensing is 0.5 nM (S/N = 3). Table 3 lists the LOD and linear range of other fluorescence sensors to detect GSH reported in recent years, where the B-CQDs exhibit the highest sensitivity [1,7,19,22,49–60]. As to the interfering experiments, the co-existing interferences, such as Na+, Cl−, S2−, Ur, UA, AA, phenolic compounds (phenol, PEA, DA), and Gly, display a negligible effect on the PL intensity with a relative error less than 2% (Fig. 6c). More interesting, the signal-off-on sensing CC and GSH by B-CQDs is reversible, as shown in Fig. 6d. The PL intensity of B-CQDs solution after quenching by 200 nM CC is recovered linearly by GSH with a range of 0–100 nM, then the same mixture exhibits a linear decrease with the further addition of CC with a range of 0–50 nM. The better cyclic performance of B-CQDs can be used as a potential fluorescent probe for the successive detection of CC and GSH.

The B-CQDs are employed for selective and sensitive detection of GSH in human serum. The quantitative recoveries of the spiked GSH range from 99.8% to 106.3% with the max RSD of 4.7%, as shown in Table 4 . The above results confirm the feasibility and reliability of the present method.

The fluorescence quenching of B-CQDs is strongly relative to the ortho-hydroxyl group (see Fig. 7 a) of CC [15], which might be oxidized by photo-electron from B-CQDs into O-benzoquinone (see inset in Fig. 7a) or complex with the B-CQDs to form complex compound. We carried out the pre-oxidization experiment of CC to transform into O-benzoquinone by the addition of H2O2 before quenching tests, as displayed in Fig. 7a. The results show that the O-benzoquinone exhibits no quenching effect, indicating that the formation of complex between CC and the B-CQDs is the reasonable process. According to the Stern-Volmer equation [61], as shown in Eq. (1),
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
⋅
[ C ]
where KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and C is the concentration of the quencher. The KSV is 1.4
×
107 M-1 from the plot of F0/F against [C] with a linear range from 1 to 50 nM. Then, the bimodular quenching constant (Kq) can be estimated to be 3.5
×
1016 M−1 s−1 from Eq. (2).
(2)
K
q
=
K
S V
τ
0
where τ0, the fluorescence lifetime of B-CQDs, is 4.0 ns (Fig. 2c). The larger Kq proves that B-CQDs quenched by CC belongs to the static quenching (>kq∼1010 M−1 s−1), suggesting ground-state non-fluorescent complexes are formed between fluorescent molecules and quenchers [61]. The fluorescence performance of the doped CQDs are governed by not only the type of guest atoms, but also by the local structure of impurities [31]. The boron hydroxyl structures (-C-B(OH)2) of B-CQDs, as confirmed by XPS, are particularly susceptible to react with ortho-hydroxyl groups of CC to form stable borate complexes [26–29]. Thereafter, the crosslink of ortho-hydroxyl group of CC with the boron hydroxyl structure (-B(OH)2) of B-CQDs can easily transfer photo-generated electrons and results in fluorescence quenching of B-CQDs.

In order to reveal the reason of fluorescence recovery sensing of GSH, the effects of reducing agent and homologous molecules are investigated. GSH is commonly used as reducing agent in the physiological system, and the resulting product is glutathione (GSSH, inset in Fig. 7b). However, no fluorescence changes are observed after addition of strong reducing agent of N2H2 and GSSH into the mixture of B-CQDs and CC, as shown in Fig. 7b. As the counterparts, the N2H2 and GSSH also add into the B-CQDs solution to verify the effect of redox reagents, and no fluorescence quenching or recovering is observed (Fig. 7b). The effects of biomolecules with similar chemical groups of GSH molecule on the PL response are performed in the mixed B-CQDs and CC solution, including the oxalic acid, Gly, C12H26S, Cys and Hcy. Interesting, only the homologous molecules of Cys and Hcy can slightly recover the fluorescence intensity, and the relative error is 6.4% and 10.5%, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7c. Compared molecular structure of Cys and Hcy with GSH in Fig. 7b and c, it is thought that the co-existence of amino and sulfhydryl groups influences the fluorescence recovery, and the recovery intensity is sensitive to the chain length between amino and sulfhydryl groups. Additionally, the fluorescence recovery intensity increases approximate two times with a double increase of GSH, as shown in Fig. 7d. Furthermore, the addition of (CC + GSH)* into the B-CQDs solution also exhibits an approximate recovery of fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 7d, suggesting that fluorescence recovery is independent of the add sequence of GSH and CC. Previous studies suggest that o-phthalaldehyde and GSH can undergo derivatization reaction to form complexes [62,63]. On the basis of the reports, a derivatization reaction of GSH and CC is speculated to form a new compound with double hexatomic-ring structure [1,62–64], as shown in Fig. 8 . Therefore, the fluorescence recovery mechanism of GSH to CC can be attributed to the release of CC from the B-CQDs by the GSH, which inhibits the photoelectron transfer from the B-CQDs to CC, therefore, fluorescence recovery occurs.

The signal-off-on sensing mechanisms of B-CQDs to CC and GSH are summarized in Fig. 8. As a fluorescence quencher, CC molecules firstly adsorb on the surface of B-CQDs due to the highly adsorption characteristic by B doping [36], then react with boric hydroxyl groups to form cis-diether groups [26–29], which crosslink the B-CQDs and CC. In this way, a photoelectron transfer system, characterized by ground-state non-fluorescent complex, forms between the CC and B-CQDs to achieve fluorescence quenching [21]. Subsequently, after the addition of GSH to the system, CC and GSH occurs the derivatization reaction to produce a stable-state double hexatomic-ring complex, releasing the B-CQDs, and final the fluorescence recovers.

In this work, we develop a sensitive fluorescent probe of B-CQDs for the detection of CC (fluorescence quenching) and GSH (fluorescence recovering). B-CQDs are synthesized by hydrothermal method with precursors of CA as C source and NaTPB as B source at 180 °C for 8 h. The as-prepared B-CQDs exhibit not only high QY (42%) but also a good stability, characterized by pH, temperature, UV illumination, and storage time. Good linear responses of PL quenching by CC and PL recovering by GSH are observed, where the linear ranges are 1–50 nM with LOD of 0.25 nM for CC and 2–100 nM with LOD of 0.5 nM for GSH, respectively. This method also has a good selectivity for real sample detection of CC in river water and GSH in human serum, where the recoveries are 103.3%–106.0% and 99.8%–106.3% for CC and GSH sensing, respectively. The signal-off-on sensing of B-CQDs opens up a new strategy of accurate, rapid and simple determination of biomolecules.

M., A.Y., Y.Y. and X. carried out the experiments. A.Y., X. F., and B. conceived and designed the experiments. B., J.H. and X.Y. contributed to data analysis and experiments design. M. and A. Y. wrote the manuscript with contributions from the other authors. All authors commented on the final manuscript and conclusions of this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants 51771121, 51572173, 51602197, and 51702212). Prof. Wang gratefully acknowledges the financial supports from Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (2019-01-07-00-07-E00015) and Shanghai Academic/Technology Research Leader Program (19XD1422900).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Sun, Y. Wang, Y. Lei, Fluorescence based explosive detection: from mechanisms to sensory materials, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (2015) 8019-8061.
